text.data-narration: The 1D Fourier Transform is a mathematical tool that decomposes a function into its frequency domain representation. It is defined as the integral of the function multiplied by a complex exponential function.

The 2D Fourier Transform is an extension of the 1D Fourier Transform to two dimensions. It is used to decompose images into their frequency domain representation. The forward transform is defined as the sum of the products of the image pixels and complex exponentials, while the inverse transform is defined as the sum of the products of the frequency domain pixels and complex exponentials.

The frequency domain representation of an image is a complex-valued function that represents the frequency components of the image. It is typically represented as the magnitude spectrum, which is the square root of the sum of the squares of the real and imaginary parts of the function. The phase spectrum is the inverse tangent of the imaginary part divided by the real part of the function.

The Fourier Transform can be visualized as a complex-valued function that represents the frequency components of an image. The resulting image can be further analyzed and manipulated using various techniques.
